plainMeaning: This verse remembers nine more great souls — teacher-monks, a renouncing king, a child-monk, and two princes — whose lives are held up as models to imitate.
recitation: ajjagiri ajjarakkhia, ajjasuhatthī udāyago maṇago | kālayasūri sambo, pajjunno mūladevo a ||

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ajja (< Skt. ārya, 'noble') + giri, a clipped form of Mahāgiri ('great mountain').
wordByWord.notes: Joint successor of Sthūlabhadra (with Suhastin), remembered for jinakalpa-like austerity. 'Giri' here is short for Mahāgiri.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ajja (< ārya) + rakkhia (< Skt. rakṣita, 'protected/guarded').
wordByWord.notes: The anuyoga-divider who organized scripture into the four anuyogas; the ancient ācārya, NOT the medieval Anchalgachchha founder of the same name.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ajja (< ārya) + suhatthī (< Skt. su-hastin, 'of good/auspicious hand').
wordByWord.notes: The other successor of Sthūlabhadra; traditionally the guru whose teaching prompted King Samprati's renunciation and temple-building.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit udāyaga (< Skt. Udāyana), nom. sg. -go; from ud-√i, 'rising'.
wordByWord.notes: King of Sindhu-Sauvīra (Vītabhaya); held in tradition as the last royal sage (carama-rājarṣi) to take dīkṣā.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit maṇaga (< Skt. Maṇaka), nom. sg. -go.
wordByWord.notes: Child-son of Śayyambhavasūri, for whom (per tradition) the Daśavaikālika was compiled; he died young after a brief, exemplary monastic life.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kālaya (< Skt. Kālaka) + sūri ('preceptor, learned teacher').
wordByWord.notes: Famous for the Gardabhilla episode and, in tradition, for shifting the Sāṁvatsarī observance; the kathā tradition knows more than one Kālaka.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit samba (< Skt. Sāmba), nom. sg. -o.
wordByWord.notes: Yādava prince, son of Kṛṣṇa Vāsudeva, who renounced under Neminātha.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pajjunna (< Skt. Pradyumna, pra-√dyut, 'to blaze forth'), nom. sg. -o.
wordByWord.notes: Son of Kṛṣṇa, who renounced and (per tradition) attained liberation.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit mūladeva (< Skt. Mūladeva), nom. sg. -o.
wordByWord.notes: Celebrated in Jain kathā (cf. Uttarādhyayana narratives) as a master of the arts and of wit who is finally won to the path.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit a, reduced form of ya/ca (< Skt. ca, 'and'), the verse-closing connective.
wordByWord.notes: Joins Mūladeva to the running list and rounds off the gāthā; metrically light.

literalTranslation: Ārya Mahāgiri, Ārya Rakṣita, Ārya Suhastin; Udāyana, Maṇaka; Kālaka-sūri, Sāmba, Pradyumna, and Mūladeva.
idiomaticTranslation: Noble Mahāgiri, noble Rakṣita, noble Suhastin; King Udāyana and the boy Maṇaka; Kālaka the preceptor, Sāmba, Pradyumna — and Mūladeva too.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the early-canon heroes of the preceding gāthā, this verse turns to the post-Mahāvīra teaching lineage and its widening reach. The three 'Ārya' names form its spine: Mahāgiri and Suhastin, the twin successors of Sthūlibhadra, set side by side as two faces of one inheritance — Mahāgiri the unbending ascetic, Suhastin the compassionate organizer who draws rulers to the Dharma; and Rakṣita, who safeguards the very transmissibility of scripture by sorting it into anuyogas. The litany then deliberately breaks rank: Udāyana shows that a crowned king can lay down the throne; Maṇaka shows that a small child, given only months in robes, can be counted among the great; Kālaka shows the ācārya who acts decisively in a crisis of the order. It closes with two Yādava princes of Kṛṣṇa's house, Sāmba and Pradyumna, and with Mūladeva, a man first famed for cleverness and pleasure who is finally turned to the path. The verse's quiet argument is breadth: noble birth and lowly trial, old monk and infant, king and prince and wit, are named in one breath, because remembrance does not rank its exemplars — it gathers them. The final particle 'a' ('and') leaves the roll-call open, carrying the reciter onward to the next gāthā rather than sealing the thought.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse quietly encodes a doctrine of the path's universality: liberation-conduct is keyed to inner resolve, not to rank, age, or prior conduct. Hence a renouncing king (Udāyana), an infant monk (Maṇaka), and a reformed pleasure-seeker (Mūladeva) stand level with the great preceptors. Ārya Rakṣita's presence touches the preservation of right knowledge (samyag-jñāna) through the anuyoga division, while Maṇaka's brief life sounds the note of impermanence (anitya) and the urgency of seizing the path now.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at pre-dawn, the verse tells the practitioner that no starting point disqualifies anyone: a king's power, a child's smallness, or a checkered past are all workable material for the path. Take one name as the day's mirror — Suhastin's reach to others, Maṇaka's urgency — and let it shape a single concrete resolve.

crossReferences.text: Daśavaikālika-sūtra ch.1 (composed in connection with Maṇaka)

sources.title: Muladeva, Mūladeva: definitions (Jainism; Uttarādhyayana references)
sources.consultedFor: Identifying Mūladeva as the kathā exemplar of wit/arts and his Uttarādhyayana attestation.

sources.title: Udayana, Udāyana: definitions (incl. Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming Udāyana as a renouncing king-figure across traditions and his Jain placement.

sources.title: Suhastisuri (Romanized)
sources.consultedFor: Ārya Suhastin's role as successor of Sthūlabhadra and his connection to King Samprati.

sources.title: Kalakacharya
sources.consultedFor: The Kālakācārya / Gardabhilla tradition and the Sāṁvatsarī-date episode.

notes: Identifications follow the standard Bharahesar reading: ajjagiri = Ārya Mahāgiri ('Giri' is a clipped form), ajjarakkhia = the ancient anuyoga-divider Ārya Rakṣita (deliberately NOT conflated with the homonymous medieval Anchalgachchha founder), ajjasuhatthī = Ārya Suhastin. The kathā tradition records more than one Kālaka, so the specific deeds attached to 'kālayasūri' vary by recension. Sāmba/Pradyumna are Kṛṣṇa's sons; Maṇaka is Śayyambhava's son. Per the preface's caution, recensions differ in roster and spelling; the verbatim Gujarati readings (incl. the connective 'અ') are preserved here and the book is treated as golden. Web 'Bharahesar Bāhubali' material was not relied upon.
